title: Inline rename for chats in the chat composite bar (Agents window)

labels.name: feature-request
labels.description: Request for new features or functionality

labels.name: verification-needed
labels.description: Verification of issue is requested

body:
Adds inline renaming of a chat directly inside its tab in the chat composite bar (Agents window), mirroring how the session title is renamed inline in the session header.

- **Double-click** a tab — or use the context-menu **Rename** — to reveal a themed input pre-filled with the chat title.
- **Enter** commits the rename; **Escape** or **blur** cancels.
- Clicking another tab while editing cancels the edit and switches to that tab.

Implemented in PR #321384.

### Verification steps
1. Open the Agents window with at least two chats so multiple tabs appear in the chat composite bar.
2. **Double-click** a tab — confirm an input appears in-place, pre-filled with the current title and fills the tab width (label, status indicator and close button hidden). Type a new name and press **Enter** — confirm the tab shows the new name.
3. Right-click a tab and choose **Rename** — confirm the same inline input appears and committing with **Enter** renames the chat.
4. Start a rename, then press **Escape** — confirm the edit is cancelled and the original title is retained. Repeat, but click outside the input (blur) — confirm the edit is also cancelled.
5. Start a rename on one tab, then click a *different* tab — confirm the edit cancels and focus switches to the clicked tab.
6. Rename a chat, then reload the Agents window — confirm the new name persists.
7. Verify in both Dark and Light themes that the rename input is themed correctly, and that long titles render without breaking the tab layout.

Refs #321384.

title: VS Code does not reload modified .ipynb files when stored on external drives and edited on another machine
body:
<!-- ⚠️⚠️ Do Not Delete This! feature_request_template ⚠️⚠️ -->
<!-- Please read our Rules of Conduct: https://opensource.microsoft.com/codeofconduct/ -->
<!-- Please search existing issues to avoid creating duplicates. -->

<!-- Describe the feature you'd like. -->
When working with .ipynb files stored on an external SSD and switching between two PCs, VS Code does not reload the updated notebook when reopening it on the first machine. Instead, it restores the old in‑memory notebook state.

To reproduce:  
1. Open a .ipynb on PC1.  
2. Close the VS Studio sesion with the .ipynb open, but saved on the external SSD
2. Modify and save it on PC2 (same file on external SSD).  
3. Return to PC1 and reopen VS Code.

I expected that VS Code detects the file changed on disk and reloads the new version, but VS Code restores the previous notebook state from memory and ignores the updated file on disk.

However, files.autoReload works for .py files but not for .ipynb; disabling hotExit and restoreWindows avoids the issue, but it is only a workaround, I think

Request:  
Please add an option for .ipynb files to always reload from disk when they change, or disable notebook state restoration.

title: sessions: inline rename for chats in the chat composite bar
body:
## What

Adds inline renaming of a chat directly inside its tab in the chat composite bar (Agents window), mirroring how the session title is renamed inline in the session header.

- **Double-click** a tab — or use the context-menu **Rename** — to reveal a themed `InputBox` pre-filled with the chat title.
- **Enter** commits via `ISessionsManagementService.renameChat`; **Escape** or **blur** cancels.
- Clicking another tab while editing cancels the edit and switches to that tab (resolved via the blur `relatedTarget`).

## How

- The rename input lives in a **stable, empty per-tab container** that is toggled via the tab's `editing` CSS class. The `InputBox` is created **lazily** only while editing and disposed when done — so no DOM is inserted/removed on demand, and tabs that are never renamed carry no widget overhead.
- The label, status indicator and close action are hidden while editing (CSS), so the input fills the tab's max width.

## Tests / screenshots

Adds a component fixture `chatCompositeBar.fixture.ts` with variants for the two-tab, mixed-status, long-title and renaming states (Dark + Light). Verified rendering locally via the Component Explorer.

## Notes for reviewers

- Changes are confined to `src/vs/sessions/` plus the new fixture and a stylelint known-variable entry; the sessions skill doc was updated with the relevant pitfalls.
- The fixture's `vs/sessions/*` imports use `eslint-disable-next-line local/code-import-patterns`, matching the existing sessions fixtures.

title: Use AsyncIterableProducer for text diff changes
body:
Refs #256854

## Why

`AsyncIterableObject` keeps an internal `_results` array of all emitted values so multiple iterators can replay the same data. The `workspace.getTextDiff` changes stream does not need that replay behavior: the diff result is produced once from the document diff promise, and `TextDiffResponse.changes` is expected to be consumed as a single async iterable.

`AsyncIterableProducer` is a better fit here because it does not retain emitted values. It passes values through its `ProducerConsumer`, which matches the one-shot nature of this stream and avoids the extra retained array.

## What

This updates `src/vs/workbench/api/common/extHost.api.impl.ts` so `getTextDiff` returns `changes` backed by `AsyncIterableProducer` instead of `AsyncIterableObject`.

The change covers:

- the async iterable import
- the cancellation path returning `EMPTY`
- the normal path creating the changes iterable

## Impact

This is a behavior-preserving refactor. API consumers still receive `AsyncIterable<TextDiffChange>` from `TextDiffResponse.changes`, and both implementations satisfy that interface.

There is no breaking API change.

## Validation

- `git diff --check -- src/vs/workbench/api/common/extHost.api.impl.ts`
- `npm run compile-check-ts-native -- --pretty false`

No new tests were added because this is a behavior-preserving implementation swap behind the existing `AsyncIterable<TextDiffChange>` API contract.

title: Handle Relative Git Worktree Paths in Worktree Detection
body:
<!-- Thank you for submitting a Pull Request. Please:
* Read our Pull Request guidelines:
  https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/wiki/How-to-Contribute#pull-requests
* Associate an issue with the Pull Request.
* Ensure that the code is up-to-date with the `main` branch.
* Include a description of the proposed changes and how to test them.
-->

Worktree auto-detection handled absolute `gitdir` entries but failed when `.git/worktrees/*/gitdir` contained relative paths (e.g. from `git worktree add --relative-paths`). This change normalizes both formats so relative-path worktrees are discovered consistently.

- **Path normalization in git worktree parsing**
  - Added `parseGitWorktreePath(gitdirPath, gitdirContent)` in `extensions/git/src/git.ts`.
  - Resolves relative `gitdir` entries against the `gitdir` file directory.
  - Normalizes separators and strips trailing `.git` (and nested suffix) for stable worktree root paths.

- **Detection flow update**
  - Replaced inline string replacement logic in `getWorktreesFS()` with the new parser.
  - Removes implicit assumption that `gitdir` content is always absolute and POSIX-shaped.

- **Focused regression coverage**
  - Added unit tests in `extensions/git/src/test/git.test.ts` for:
    - relative `gitdir` paths
    - absolute `gitdir` paths

```ts
const worktreeGitPath = path.isAbsolute(gitdirContent)
	? gitdirContent
	: path.resolve(path.dirname(gitdirPath), gitdirContent);

return worktreeGitPath.replace(/[\\/]\.git(?:[\\/].*)?$/, '');
```

This supplements (and for copilot settings, will eventually supplant) the device configuration specified in our [documentation](https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/enterprise/policies) The supported keys for now (matching a shared spec with the copilot surfaces): | Managed-setting key | Type | Example | Policy | |---|---|---|---| | `permissions.disableBypassPermissionsMode` | string | `disable` | `ChatToolsAutoApprove` | | `enabledPlugins` | JSON string | `{"my-plugin@agent-skills":true,"old@acme":false}` | `ChatEnabledPlugins` | | `extraKnownMarketplaces` | JSON string | `{"agent-skills":"github/agent-skills","acme":"acme/things#main"}` | `ChatExtraMarketplaces` | | `strictKnownMarketplaces` | boolean | `true` | `ChatStrictMarketplaces` | # Task **Validate for your platform that the device configuration can be manually installed and is honored by VS Code.** Validate that the provided managed-keys configure the settings appropriately (use `@hasPolicy`): ## macOS Follow the [steps in our docs](https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/enterprise/policies#_configure-policies-manually) to install the mobileconfig file shown below. Feel free to edit it. #### copilot.mobileconfig ``` PayloadContent PayloadDisplayName GitHub Copilot Managed Settings PayloadIdentifier com.github.copilot.D1C16CCE-E9E9-47CE-A6FD-A989BEBB2E16 PayloadType com.github.copilot PayloadUUID D1C16CCE-E9E9-47CE-A6FD-A989BEBB2E16 PayloadVersion 1 permissions.disableBypassPermissionsMode disable enabledPlugins {"assign-issue-to-copilot@agent-skills":true,"deprecated-plugin@acme-things":false} extraKnownMarketplaces {"agent-skills":"github/agent-skills","acme-things":"acme/things#main","internal-https":"https://git.example.com/marketplace.git","internal-ssh":"ssh://git@git.example.com/marketplace.git#v1.2.0"} strictKnownMarketplaces PayloadDescription Full-coverage GitHub Copilot managed settings: permissions, enabled plugins, extra known marketplaces, and strict marketplace enforcement. For testing native MDM ↔ server managed-settings parity. PayloadDisplayName GitHub Copilot Managed Settings (All Cases) PayloadIdentifier com.github.copilot PayloadOrganization Microsoft PayloadType Configuration PayloadUUID B89E2663-3287-4BA5-9601-37235BEA3A3D PayloadVersion 1 TargetDeviceType 5 ``` ## Windows 1. Follow the [steps in our docs](https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/enterprise/policies#_step-1-install-the-policy-definition-files) to install the `GitHubCopilot.adm{x,l}` files below into the right directory (follow our existing docs as a pointer). 2. Open 'Local Group Policy Editor' and configure settings #### GitHubCopilot.admx ```xml disable ``` #### en-us\GitHubCopilot.adml ``` GitHub Copilot Managed Settings Administrative template for GitHub Copilot enterprise-managed settings in Visual Studio Code. GitHub Copilot Disable bypass-permissions ("YOLO") mode Controls Copilot's global auto-approve, also known as "YOLO mode". When Enabled, global auto-approve is turned off for all tools in all workspaces and cannot be re-enabled by the user. When Disabled or Not Configured, the user's own setting applies. Corresponds to the managed setting "permissions.disableBypassPermissionsMode" = "disable" (registry value name: permissions.disableBypassPermissionsMode, REG_SZ). Trust only enterprise-supplied plugin marketplaces Controls which plugin marketplaces Copilot trusts. When Enabled, only marketplaces supplied via enterprise policy are trusted; plugins from any other marketplace will not load. When Disabled or Not Configured, user-supplied marketplaces are also trusted. Corresponds to the managed setting "strictKnownMarketplaces" (registry value name: strictKnownMarketplaces, REG_DWORD; 1 = enabled, 0 = disabled). Enabled plugins Enterprise-managed plugin enablement. Provide a JSON object whose keys are plugin IDs in <plugin>@<marketplace> form and whose values are true (enabled) or false (disabled). When set, only plugins mapped to true are allowed to load. Invalid JSON is ignored. Additional plugin marketplaces Additional enterprise-managed plugin marketplaces, unioned with the user's own marketplaces. Provide a JSON object whose keys are marketplace names and whose values are GitHub shorthand (owner/repo or owner/repo#ref) or Git URIs (<url> or <url>#ref). title: Fix media preview cache-busting version
body:
Fixes #320928

Media previews currently append `Date.now()` to resource URIs on every render. In web, those resources are cached by the service worker using the full request URL, so reopening the same unchanged media file creates a new cache key each time.

This changes media previews to derive the cache-busting version from the resource stat (`mtime` and `size`) instead. The version stays stable while the resource is unchanged, but changes when the resource changes. If the stat cannot be read, the preview falls back to the resource identity so the webview can still render its normal load error instead of failing before HTML generation.

The shared helper is used by image, audio, and video previews.

Verification:
- `PATH="/opt/homebrew/opt/node@24/bin:$PATH" npm --prefix extensions/media-preview run compile`
- `PATH="/opt/homebrew/opt/node@24/bin:$PATH" npx mocha extensions/media-preview/test/mediaResourceVersion.test.js --ui tdd`
- `PATH="/opt/homebrew/opt/node@24/bin:$PATH" npm run compile-client`
- `git diff --check`

title: Avoid stale simple dialog folder updates
body:
Fixes #152089

This fixes a race in the simple file dialog when a slower folder update is canceled by a newer one. Previously, the canceled update could still resume after `fileService.resolve` and write `currentFolder` / `userEnteredPathSegment` before checking the cancellation token. That stale state could then affect subsequent input handling on slow file system providers.

The update now computes the next folder state locally and only commits it after item creation finishes and the token is still active. `createBackItem` also uses the folder passed into `createItems` instead of reading `this.currentFolder`, so the current folder does not need to be mutated before the async item work completes.

Verification:
- `PATH="/opt/homebrew/opt/node@24/bin:$PATH" npm run compile-client`

title: Allow simple file dialog to create nested folders
body:
Fixes #320447

This updates the simple file dialog folder-only open flow to allow creating a nested missing folder path when the nearest existing ancestor is a writable directory. The actual creation still uses `fileService.createFolder`, which already performs recursive mkdir.

Validation now walks up the missing path, checking each missing basename before prompting to create the requested folder.

title: fix: AuthChallengeBadToken on concurrent session restore
body:
<!-- Thank you for submitting a Pull Request. Please:
* Read our Pull Request guidelines:
  https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/wiki/How-to-Contribute#pull-requests
* Associate an issue with the Pull Request.
* Ensure that the code is up-to-date with the `main` branch.
* Include a description of the proposed changes and how to test them.
-->

Restoring multiple remote SSH workspace sessions concurrently fails with `AuthChallengeBadToken` due to a race condition. VS Code doesn't use the cached `_connections` until they're successfully connected, which lead to multiple connection creations simultaneously.

My proposed fix adds a new `_pendingConnects` map with the pending connections that serves as a cache similar to the existing `_connections` map, so only one connection is attempted at once.

Fixes #249861

title: Test: Agent Feedback with Agent Host Providers
body:
Refs: #321254

- [x] anyOS @federicobrancasi
- [ ] anyOS @dileepyavan

Complexity: 4


[Create Issue](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/new?template=blank&body=Testing+%23321338%0A%0A&assignees=benibenj)

---

The agent host now exposes **server-side feedback ("comments") tools** (`addComment`, `listComments`, `deleteComments`, `resolveComments`) and a built-in **`act-on-feedback`** skill. When you submit feedback on a session's changes, the agent loop uses the skill + tools to read your comments, make the requested edits, and mark the comments resolved. This works for any (codex not yet) agent-host harness, verify it for **both Copilot CLI and Claude**.

## Setup

1. Open a folder with some real code (a Git repo is ideal so changes are easy to see).
2. Make sure **both providers run through the agent host** and appear in the Agents window's new-session picker:
   - **Claude**: set `github.copilot.chat.claudeAgent.enabled: true` and `chat.agents.claude.preferAgentHost: true`
   - **Copilot CLI**: ensure the Copilot CLI agent-host provider is enabled/available.

## Test: run the whole flow once per harness (Copilot CLI, then Claude)

1. Open the **Agents window** and start a new session with the chosen provider.
2. Ask the agent to make a code change.
3. **Open the session's changes** (the changed files / diff).
4. In a changed file, **select a range of code** and **add a feedback comment** describing what to change
   - Verify the comment renders as an agent-feedback comment anchored to that range.
5. Add **2–3 more comments** on different selections/files
6. Click **Submit Feedback**

### Verify

- [ ] The agent invokes the built-in **`act-on-feedback`** skill and calls the **`listComments`** tool to retrieve your comments (visible as a tool call in the session).
- [ ] The feedback tools run **without a confirmation prompt** (they're auto-approved).
- [ ] The agent makes edits that actually address each comment.
- [ ] When done, the agent calls **`resolveComments`** and the addressed comments **disappear from the UI**.
- [ ] The resulting code changes match what each comment asked for.

title: Feature Request: Respect metered network connections on Linux (NetworkManager)

labels.name: bug
labels.description: Issue identified by VS Code Team member as probable bug

labels.name: network
labels.description: Network related issues

body:
<!-- ⚠️⚠️ Do Not Delete This! feature_request_template ⚠️⚠️ -->
<!-- Please read our Rules of Conduct: https://opensource.microsoft.com/codeofconduct/ -->
<!-- Please search existing issues to avoid creating duplicates. -->

<!-- Describe the feature you'd like. -->

# Feature Request: Respect metered network connections on Linux (NetworkManager)

## Problem

VS Code performs automatic update checks and extension update downloads regardless of whether the active network connection is marked as metered. On Linux, NetworkManager exposes this information system-wide via `connection.metered`, which applications are expected to respect to reduce background network activity on limited connections (mobile hotspots, tethered phones, satellite links, expensive roaming).

VS Code does not detect or respect this setting. In my opinion, the Auto-Update should be disabled when on an metered network.

## Expected Behavior

When the active connection is metered, VS Code should:

- suspend automatic update downloads, **or**
- offer a setting to disable update activity on metered connections

The auto check for new updates should be still possible, because it should not use huge amount of data. 
This should apply to VS Code application updates, extension update checks, and extension auto-downloads.

## Linux Integration

NetworkManager exposes the metered status via:

```bash
nmcli connection show <connection-name> | grep connection.metered
```

Values: `yes`, `no`, `unknown`. GNOME, KDE, Manjaro, Ubuntu, and Fedora all surface this in their network settings UI.

## Suggested Implementation

Detect `connection.metered` on Linux and pause background update activity automatically. Alternatively, expose a user setting:

```json
"update.respectMeteredConnections": true
```

- VS Code Version:

Version: 1.124.2 (Universal)
Commit: 6928394f91b684055b873eecb8bc281365131f1c

ElectronBuildId: 14159160
Chromium: 148.0.7778.97
Node.js: 24.15.0
V8: 14.8.178.14-electron.0
OS: Darwin arm64 27.0.0

(Note: This also used to happen on the previous macOS version 26 before I upgraded to 27.)

Steps to Reproduce:

I'm not entirely sure about the exact trigger, but this seems to happen on macOS when VS Code is in full-screen mode and left idle in the background for a while—specifically after I switch to another virtual desktop or when the computer wakes up from sleep. The terminal layout getting messed up has happened to me quite a few times, but this is the first time I've encountered that solid white block in the Sticky Scroll area shown in the video.

title: Enable search in current and past conversation
body:
### Description

current only place user can search in in session title via the side "session" pane, but as conversation gets long would be very useful to support ctrl+F search in the chat window (current session past history) and in the "session" tab search through past conversation content in addition to title. Claude code supports this and is very useful

### Issue Details

Item|Value
---|---
Issue Category|Feature Request
Target|GitHub Copilot Chat
VS Code Version|Code 1.124.2 (6928394f91b684055b873eecb8bc281365131f1c, 2026-06-12T00:12:36Z)
OS Version|Windows_NT x64 10.0.26200

Extension Version|0.52.0

title: Advanced Autopilot goal evaluation receives "Start implementation" instead of the actual user request from Plan mode
body:
## Summary
When `chat.autopilot.advanced.enabled` is true, after Plan mode generates a plan, clicking "Implement with Autopilot" starts a new Autopilot session. The goal evaluation step only receives `Original user request: Start implementation` instead of the actual user requirement from the Plan turn.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Enable `chat.autopilot.advanced.enabled`
2. Ask Copilot Chat a question (e.g., "Add version and release date columns to the report")
3. Click "Plan" → let Plan agent respond with a plan
4. Click "Implement with Autopilot"
5. After Autopilot finishes, check the goal evaluation prompt

## Expected
Goal evaluation prompt should contain the actual user request from the Plan turn.

## Actual
Goal evaluation prompt only contains:
```
Original user request:
Start implementation
```

## Impact
The goal evaluation LLM has no context about what the user wanted, so it blindly returns YES, making the feature useless.

title: Bug: Advanced Autopilot goal evaluation only gets "Start implementation" as original request
body:
When `chat.autopilot.advanced.enabled` is true, after Plan mode generates a plan, clicking "Implement with Autopilot" starts a new Autopilot session. The goal evaluation step at the end sends the LLM a prompt like:

```
Original user request:
Start implementation

Conversation transcript:
… (earlier turns truncated)

rvice\\update.py","oldString":"@dataclass(frozen=True)\nclass SlugMigrationResult:\n    slug: str\n    status: str\n    na… (truncated 481 chars))
  Assistant response: (empty)

… (conversation history starting from "Start implementation")

Has the original request been fully completed?
```

The user's actual requirement is only documented in the Plan agent's response from the previous conversation turn. The Autopilot evaluation session only sees `Start implementation` as the original request, so the goal evaluation LLM has no idea what the user actually wanted — it's guessing blindly and almost always returning YES, making the feature useless.

**Suggested fix:** Include the actual user request from the Plan mode turn in the goal evaluation prompt.

title: Bump esbuild from 0.27.2 to 0.28.1 in /extensions

labels.name: javascript
labels.description: JavaScript support issues

labels.name: dependencies
labels.description: Pull requests that update a dependency file

body:
Bumps [esbuild](https://github.com/evanw/esbuild) from 0.27.2 to 0.28.1.
Release notes

Sourced from esbuild's releases.

v0.28.1

  • Disallow \ in local development server HTTP requests (GHSA-g7r4-m6w7-qqqr)

    This release fixes a security issue where HTTP requests to esbuild's local development server could traverse outside of the serve directory on Windows using a \ backslash character. It happened due to the use of Go's path.Clean() function, which only handles Unix-style / characters. HTTP requests with paths containing \ are no longer allowed.

    Thanks to @​dellalibera for reporting this issue.

  • Add integrity checks to the Deno API (GHSA-gv7w-rqvm-qjhr)

    The previous release of esbuild added integrity checks to esbuild's npm install script. This release also adds integrity checks to esbuild's Deno install script. Now esbuild's Deno API will also fail with an error if the downloaded esbuild binary contains something other than the expected content.

    Note that esbuild's Deno API installs from registry.npmjs.org by default, but allows the NPM_CONFIG_REGISTRY environment variable to override this with a custom package registry. This change means that the esbuild executable served by NPM_CONFIG_REGISTRY must now match the expected content.

    Thanks to @​sondt99 for reporting this issue.

  • Avoid inlining using and await using declarations (#4482)

    Previously esbuild's minifier sometimes incorrectly inlined using and await using declarations into subsequent uses of that declaration, which then fails to dispose of the resource correctly. This bug happened because inlining was done for let and const declarations by avoiding doing it for var declarations, which no longer worked when more declaration types were added. Here's an example:

    // Original code
{
  using x = new Resource()
  x.activate()
}

    // Old output (with --minify)
    
new Resource().activate();
    

    // New output (with --minify)
    
{using e=new Resource;e.activate()}

  • Fix module evaluation when an error is thrown (#4461, #4467)

    If an error is thrown during module evaluation, esbuild previously didn't preserve the state of the module for subsequent module references. This was observable if import() or require() is used to import a module multiple times. The thrown error is supposed to be thrown by every call to import() or require(), not just the first. With this release, esbuild will now throw the same error every time you call import() or require() on a module that throws during its evaluation.

  • Fix some edge cases around the new operator (#4477)

    Previously esbuild incorrectly printed certain edge cases involving complex expressions inside the target of a new expression (specifically an optional chain and/or a tagged template literal). The generated code for the new target was not correctly wrapped with parentheses, and either contained a syntax error or had different semantics. These edge cases have been fixed so that they now correctly wrap the new target in parentheses. Here is an example of some affected code:

    // Original code
new (foo()`bar`)()
new (foo()?.bar)()

    // Old output
    
new foo()bar();
    
new (foo())?.bar();

... (truncated)

Changelog

Sourced from esbuild's changelog.

Changelog: 2025

This changelog documents all esbuild versions published in the year 2025 (versions 0.25.0 through 0.27.2).

Commits
  • bb9db84 publish 0.28.1 to npm
  • 9ff053e security: add integrity checks to the Deno API
  • 0a9bf21 enforce non-negative size in gzip parser
  • e2a1a71 security: forbid \\ in local dev server requests
  • 83a2cbf fix #4482: don't inline using declarations
  • 308ad74 fix #4471: renaming of nested var declarations
  • f013f5f fix some typos
  • aafd6e4 chore: fix some minor issues in comments (#4462)
  • 15300c3 follow up: cjs evaluation fixes
  • 1bda0c3 fix #4461, fix #4467: esm evaluation fixes
  • Additional commits viewable in compare view

<!-- 🔧 Launch with `code --disable-extensions` to check. -->
Does this issue occur when all extensions are disabled?: Yes

- VS Code Version: 1.123.0 and 1.124.0 (1.122.1 does NOT reproduce the issue)
- OS Version: CachyOS (Arch Linux based), kernel 7.0.12-1-cachyos, tested on both Wayland and X11 (--ozone-platform=x11)

Steps to Reproduce:
1. Open VS Code (does not matter if a project is opened)
2. Trigger any action that opens a native confirmation dialog via DialogService#prompt, e.g.:
   - Help > About
   - Source Control > Sync (with git.confirmSync enabled)
   - Source Control > Discard Changes on a modified file
3. The entire window immediately becomes unresponsive (after a while turns grey/blank) as soon as the dialog is requested, and never recovers. VS Code must be force-killed.

What I expected: the dialog opens normally and the window remains responsive.

What actually happens: the whole window freezes the instant the dialog is requested.

Additional notes:
- Reproduced with extensions fully disabled (--disable-extensions), confirming this is not extension-related.
- Reproduced on both Wayland and XWayland (--ozone-platform=x11), so it does not appear to be Wayland-specific.
- Bisected via local package cache: 1.122.1-1.1 works correctly. 1.123.0-1.1 and 1.124.0-1.1 both reproduce the freeze.
- Git extension log (Git.log) shows the operation reaching the dialog and then nothing further:
  `DialogService#prompt This action will pull and push commits from and to "origin/<branch>"`
  immediately followed by repeated native crashpad errors:
  `[ERROR:third_party/crashpad/crashpad/snapshot/elf/elf_dynamic_array_reader.h:64] tag not found`
  `[ERROR:third_party/crashpad/crashpad/util/process/process_memory_range.cc:75] read out of range`
- Attempting to attach gdb/strace to the frozen main process (code/code.mjs) causes it to crash with SIGSEGV immediately on PTRACE_ATTACH/PTRACE_SEIZE, preventing further inspection this way.
- No additional errors appear in Dev Tools Console (Help > Toggle Developer Tools) before the freeze; the last console output is the DialogService#prompt trace line above.

Workaround: downgrading to 1.122.1 resolves the issue completely.

title: TextMate tokenizer enters incorrect scope after typeof a < "u" in JavaScript, affecting subsequent function declarations
body: ## VS Code Version ```text Version: 1.124.2 (user setup) Commit: 6928394f91b684055b873eecb8bc281365131f1c Date: 2026-06-12T00:12:36Z Electron: 42.2.0 ElectronBuildId: 14159160 Chromium: 148.0.7778.97 Node.js: 24.15.0 V8: 14.8.178.14-electron.0 OS: Windows_NT x64 10.0.26200 ``` **Fresh install of VS Code with all default settings. No extensions installed, no custom configuration applied.** **Also reproduced on VS Code for the Web ([vscode.dev](https://vscode.dev/)) with no extensions or custom configuration.** ## Reproducible Example ```js function bar() { return "bar"; } var a = typeof a < "u"; function foo() { return "foo"; } ``` ## Steps to Reproduce 1. Install VS Code with all default settings (no extensions, no custom configuration). 2. Create a new `.js` file. 3. Paste the code above. 4. Run: ```text Developer: Inspect Editor Tokens and Scopes ``` 5. Inspect the first `function` keyword (`bar` declaration). 6. Inspect the second `function` keyword (`foo` declaration). ## Actual Result The first function declaration is tokenized correctly: ```text storage.type.function.js meta.function.js source.js ``` The second function declaration is tokenized incorrectly: ```text entity.name.type.js meta.type.parameters.js meta.var.expr.js source.js ``` Syntax highlighting becomes incorrect for all code following: ```js var a = typeof a < "u"; ``` ## Expected Result The second function declaration should receive the same scopes as the first: ```text storage.type.function.js meta.function.js source.js ``` No tokenizer state should leak past the end of the variable declaration. ## Additional Investigation Replacing: ```js var a = typeof a < "u"; ``` with: ```js var a = typeof a > "u"; ``` immediately fixes the issue. The second `function` is then correctly scoped as: ## Notes - Reproduced on a fresh VS Code install with all default settings and no extensions. - Reproduced across all built-in themes; the issue is not theme-specific. - The JavaScript is syntactically valid.

title: Image carousel - refresh when new images are added while open (#302008)
body:
# Image carousel - refresh when new images are added while open

Fixes #302008

## Problem

When an agent keeps producing screenshots while the **Images Preview** carousel is open, the new images don't appear until you close and reopen it. The carousel collected the chat images once at open time and its collection was immutable; being a modal editor, it blocks input while the agent keeps streaming images behind it.

## Fix

Drive a live refresh from the chat side (which owns the data) into the editor:

- **`imageCarouselEditorInput.ts`** — the collection is now updatable via `onDidChangeCollection` + `updateCollection()` (mirrors the existing `setName()` / `_onDidChangeLabel` pattern).
- **`imageCarouselEditor.ts`** — listens and refreshes in place, keeping the viewed image focused by id.
- **`imageCarousel.contribution.ts`** — the open command returns the editor input.
- **`chatImageCarouselService.ts`** — watches `viewModel.onDidChange` (debounced), re-collects, and pushes via `updateCollection` only when the set of image ids changed; tears down on the input's `onWillDispose`.

chat → image-carousel is `import type` only; the editor doesn't import chat. Single-image carousels are unaffected.

**Refresh is in place and cheap.** When images are only appended (the common case), the editor adds just the new thumbnails — the container isn't cleared and the main image element isn't recreated, so there's no flicker and the viewed image/zoom stay put. Any other change falls back to a full rebuild. Image bytes are read from disk once per session and each image is decoded once, so refreshes stay cheap as the count grows.

## Tests

- **live refresh** — images streamed across ticks push one deduped update; a text-only change pushes none; updates stop after close.
- **live refresh (multiple chats)** — the single (singleton) service handles two chats; each opens on its first image and only the active carousel refreshes.
- **createCachingReadFile** — the per-session byte cache reads each image once.

25 passing across ChatImageCarouselService.

## How to test

1. In chat agent mode, run a task that emits several screenshots over time.
2. Click the first screenshot to open the carousel; keep it open.
3. New screenshots appear as thumbnails automatically, and the image you were viewing stays put.

Before:

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/e2e54930-ce9b-478f-aae0-e229613d1d6f

After:

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/52f3202a-64fd-42fd-87f9-3677f79dc4cb

title: Git operations provide no indication when SSH/FIDO authentication requires user interaction
body:
VS Code Git operations such as push, pull, fetch, or sync can appear to hang when SSH authentication is waiting for user interaction.

This is particularly noticeable when using FIDO/U2F security keys, where the user may need to touch the security key to continue authentication. However, VS Code currently does not clearly indicate that user interaction is required.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Configure Git to use SSH authentication with a FIDO/U2F security key.
2. Perform a Git operation such as push or pull from VS Code.
3. When authentication requires touching the security key, observe the VS Code UI.

## Actual Behavior

The Git operation appears to be waiting or hanging, with little indication that user interaction is required.

## Expected Behavior

VS Code should provide a clearer notification or progress message when SSH authentication is waiting for user interaction, helping users understand that they may need to confirm authentication or touch their security key.

## Additional Context

This can be confusing for users unfamiliar with FIDO-based SSH authentication, as the operation may appear stalled even though authentication is simply awaiting confirmation.

Related pull request:
https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/pull/319747

title: # [Tokenomics] Agent system prompt and tool schemas are written for humans

labels.name: new release
labels.description: Issues found in a recent release of VS Code

body:
- Copilot Chat Extension Version: 0.52.0
- VS Code Version: 1.124.2
- OS Version: macOS 26.5
- Feature: agent mode
- Selected model: all models (cost issue, model-agnostic)
- Logs: N/A

Steps to Reproduce:

1. Send any agent request
2. ~13,900 tokens of system prompt + tool schema overhead are billed before the model processes a character of user input

# [Token Economics] Agent system prompt and tool schemas are written for humans — ~13,900t of billable overhead per request

With usage-based billing, every token in the system prompt is a direct user cost. The instruction blocks and tool schemas are authored as English prose documentation. Models don't read — they tokenize. Same behavioral constraints extract from directives at 80% less cost.

## Numbers (from live bundle, `extension.js` + `package.json`)

**Instruction blocks — fire every request:**

| Block | Current | Target | Saving |
|-------|:-------:|:------:|:------:|
| `<instructions>` | 520t | 150t | 370t |
| `<toolUseInstructions>` | 627t | 180t | 447t |
| `<editFileInstructions>` | 529t | 130t | 399t |
| **Subtotal** | **1,676t** | **460t** | **1,216t** |

**Tool schemas (38 tools, `contributes.languageModelTools`):**

| Component | Tokens |
|-----------|:------:|
| Structure (names, types, required) | 1,609t |
| `modelDescription` prose | 5,166t |
| Parameter `description` prose | 2,456t |
| Other fields (displayName, tags, legacyNames…) | 2,968t |
| **Total minified / pretty-printed** | **12,199t / 14,508t** |

**Unit economics (Claude Sonnet 4.5, ~$3/M input tokens):**

| | Current | After |
|--|:-------:|:-----:|
| Overhead tokens/request | ~13,900t | ~2,760t |
| Cost/request (overhead only) | ~$0.042 | ~$0.008 |
| Saving/request | | **~$0.034** |

At 1M requests/day: **~$1M/month** billed to users for tokens with zero instruction value.

## What's wasteful

1. **Prose instead of directives.** `copilot_replaceString` modelDescription: 311t. Agent-equivalent: 38t. Same constraints.
2. **Duplicate rules.** SearchSubagent/ExecutionSubagent routing rules appear 3× (once in `<instructions>`, twice in `<toolUseInstructions>`). ~208t waste.
3. **Cross-layer duplication.** The "3-5 lines context" rule for `replace_string_in_file` is stated in: (1) `modelDescription` (311t), (2) `oldString` param desc (98t), (3) `<editFileInstructions>` (~529t). All three say the same thing.
4. **Whitespace.** If schemas are serialized pretty-printed before reaching the API: 2,309t of pure whitespace. One-liner fix.
5. **Unnecessary examples.** `<editFileInstructions>` includes a full `Person` class example (~47t). The prose rule above it is sufficient.
6. **`AlternateGPTPrompt` has no shared base** with `DefaultAgentPrompt` — every instruction block is duplicated in source, maintenance doubles, compression work has to be done twice.

## Proposed fix (before/after for `copilot_replaceString`)

**Current (510t):**
```json
{
  "modelDescription": "This is a tool for making edits in an existing file in the workspace. For moving or renaming files, use run in terminal tool with the 'mv' command instead. For larger edits, split them into smaller edits and call the edit tool multiple times to ensure accuracy. Before editing, always ensure you have the context to understand the file's contents and context. To edit a file, provide: 1) filePath (absolute path), 2) oldString (MUST be the exact literal text...",
  "inputSchema": { "properties": { "oldString": { "description": "The exact literal text to replace, preferably unescaped. For single replacements (default), include at least 3 lines of context BEFORE and AFTER the target text, matching whitespace and indentation precisely..." } } }
}
```

**Agent-optimized (80t):**
```json
{
  "modelDescription": "Single string replacement. oldString=exact literal+3-5 lines context, unique match required. mv/rename→terminal. Large edits→split calls.",
  "inputSchema": { "properties": { "oldString": { "description": "Exact literal text to replace. Include 3-5 lines context before/after. Must match exactly once." } } }
}
```

## Remediation (ordered by effort/impact, all zero-behavior-change)

1. **Minify schemas before sending to model API.** 2,309t. One-liner. Zero risk.
2. **Remove duplicate subagent routing rules from `<instructions>`.** ~208t. Keep in `<toolUseInstructions>`.
3. **Remove `Person` class example.** ~47t.
4. **Rewrite instruction blocks as directives** (520+627+529t → 150+180+130t). PRs welcome — proposed rewrites in comments below.
5. **Rewrite `modelDescription` fields as calling-constraint directives.** Top 8 tools alone: ~2,224t saving.
6. **Compress param descriptions.** `copilot_findTextInFiles` params: 306t → target <80t.
7. **Extract shared base for `DefaultAgentPrompt`/`AlternateGPTPrompt`.** Prevents double-maintenance.

**Authoring principle going forward:** tool descriptions carry calling constraints only. Behavioral routing rules live in the system prompt once. No rule in more than one place.

## Files
- `extensions/copilot/src/extension/prompts/node/agent/defaultAgentInstructions.tsx`
- `extensions/copilot/package.json` → `contributes.languageModelTools`

title: Test: Browser in Remote Workspaces

labels.name: browser-integration
labels.description: Web browsing features integrated into VS Code (e.g. integrated browser)

body: Refs: https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/293229 - [ ] macOS @osortega - [x] windows @hediet - [ ] linux @eleanorjboyd Complexity: 5 Roles: Developer, Engineering Manager [Create Issue](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/new?template=blank&body=Testing+%23321294%0A%0A&assignees=kycutler) --- The integrated browser now supports proxying web requests (`http` / `https`) via remote workspace connections. There are two aspects of this feature being tested: 1. The proxy server (runs within the local machine; may have OS-specific quirks) 2. The remote connections (established by the remote extension; e.g. WSL / SSH / Dev Containers / etc.) Thus, for each OS **please verify against several types of remotes** and include the remote connection type you used in any reported issues. Some examples: - Tunnels - SSH - Dev Containers - WSL - Codespaces > [!IMPORTANT] > **When you are done, please comment which remote types you verified against so we know what has been covered.** > > Don't feel pressured to test every remote type out there, but please do test **2 or 3 at minimum** and more if you have the capacity. ## Setup - Enable the setting `workbench.browser.enableRemoteProxy`. - Ensure the setting `workbench.browser.dataStorage` is not set to `global` (`default` is ideal). - Install any remote extensions you need to test with. The [Remote Development Extension Pack](https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=ms-vscode-remote.vscode-remote-extensionpack) is a good place to start. Testing would best easiest with a workspace that contains some kind of web app. ## Non-Remote-Specific Scenarios These can just be tested within one remote workspace; no need to check them across various remote types. ### Remote Indicator When remote proxying is enabled, the browser URL bar should show a remote indicator: This becomes disabled in a few scenarios such as when using a globally scoped session. Otherwise it should stay active whenever proxying is active. ### Localhost Links When clicking a link, such as in a terminal output, the **original** / remote URL should be opened, if remote proxying is enabled. If you disable the `workbench.browser.enableRemoteProxy` setting, and open the link again, it should open the **forwarded** / local URL (this is existing behavior). ### `file://` URLs File URLs are not currently proxied. When opening a `file://` URL in a proxied tab, the indicator should turn into a warning: ### Agent-opened tabs Verify that browser tabs opened by an agent are also proxied, and open at the correct (remote) URL. ### Globally scoped sessions If the `workbench.browser.dataStorage` setting is `global`, newly opened tabs (using the global session) **should not** be proxied. This prevents tabs in other open workspaces from getting proxied too. ## Remote-Specific Scenarios Please check these scenarios for each remote type you verify. Exploratory testing here is also encouraged. `http` and `https` operate via different code paths; please treat them independently and mention which you were using when filing any issues. ### Local / HTTP - Connect to a localhost HTTP server (that is only on the remote and not exposed on the local machine). Confirm it works well and any resources load as expected. - Try connecting to a localhost port that is not open. It should fail quickly and with an understandable error message (though it will be different than the "standard" error due to the proxying -- this is unavoidable). ### Web / HTTPS - Connect to any public site (e.g. Google, Wikipedia, etc). Confirm it works well and you can browse the web without issues. - Try connecting to an https host that does not exist (e.g. `https://asdjkfgasldjfglkjasdgf.com`). It should fail quickly and with an understandable error message (though it will be different than the "standard" error due to the proxying -- this is unavoidable).

title: Fix OS_ID parsing from /etc/os-release
body:
Fixes #232159

The `check-requirements-linux.sh` script incorrectly parsed the `OS_ID` from `/etc/os-release` when quotes were present (e.g. `ID="rocky"`), causing devcontainer setup ad-hoc parsing failures on distributions like Rocky 8.5. 

This pull request modifies the script to source `/etc/os-release` using the POSIX standard `.` operator, safely handling quotes and extracting the correct `$ID` value.

title: Fix webview resource loading detach in Safari/WebKit
body:
fyi @mjbvz this is for https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/316841 -- looks like if this code got ahold of a slice of a larger buffer, it would send off the entire buffer... this would cause other code to throw when it tried to use other pieces of the larger buffer.

---
🤖 

## Context

Fixes the `Underlying ArrayBuffer has been detached from the view or out-of-bounds: subarray` error seen in web/remote mode on Safari/WebKit.

Related: microsoft/vscode#316841, microsoft/vscode#314126

## Problem

When transferable streams are unsupported (Safari/WebKit), webview resource chunks are sent via `postMessage` with a transfer list. The chunk was wrapped as a `Uint8Array` view over its **entire** underlying `ArrayBuffer`, and that whole `ArrayBuffer` was transferred:

```ts
const data = new Uint8Array(chunk.buffer.buffer, chunk.buffer.byteOffset, chunk.buffer.byteLength);
this._send('did-load-resource-chunk', { id, data }, [data.buffer]);
```

In web/remote mode the chunk is frequently a **view aliasing a larger, shared `ArrayBuffer`** (e.g. a message buffer sliced into many views by the IPC deserialize pipeline). Transferring that shared buffer detaches **every sibling view** still in use. WebKit detaches synchronously, so the next IPC read throws `Underlying ArrayBuffer has been detached from the view` and webview resource loading breaks. V8 (Chrome/Edge/desktop) detaches lazily, which is why this reproduces on Safari/iOS only.

## Fix

Copy the chunk into a freshly-owned `ArrayBuffer` (sized to exactly the chunk window) before transferring it, so the transfer can only ever detach the copy:

```ts
const data = chunk.buffer.slice();
this._send('did-load-resource-chunk', { id, data }, [data.buffer]);
```

`Uint8Array.prototype.slice` returns an exact-window copy backed by its own `ArrayBuffer`, leaving the shared source intact.

title: Chat session marked empty and hidden while Copilot transcript/edit state remain intact
body:
## Summary

A long GitHub Copilot Chat agent session disappeared from the VS Code/Copilot Chat history list after an interruption, even though the full Copilot transcript and chat edit-state data are still present on disk and parse cleanly.

The local storage appears to be in a split-brain state:

- VS Code's chat session index says the session is empty.
- The UI-facing `chatSessions/<sessionId>.jsonl` file has been reduced to a tiny empty-session stub.
- The Copilot transcript for the same session contains hundreds of valid events.
- The `chatEditingSessions/<sessionId>/state.json` file still exists and contains edit checkpoints/file state.

This made the session unavailable from the UI and prevented normal continuation, even though the work is forensically recoverable from the transcript.

## Impact

This is high impact for long agent-mode coding sessions. The affected session contained hours of work, user decisions, tool results, and in-progress debugging state. The user reports this has happened more than once, so it does not look like a one-off user mistake.

At minimum, VS Code/Copilot Chat should avoid hiding a session when other local session artifacts prove it is non-empty, or offer a recovery path from the transcript/edit-state files.

## Environment

- OS: Windows, x64
- VS Code: `1.124.2`
- VS Code commit: `6928394f91b684055b873eecb8bc281365131f1c`
- Copilot Chat metadata from the surviving transcript/debug log:
  - `producer`: `copilot-agent`
  - `copilotVersion`: `0.52.0`
  - `vscodeVersion`: `1.124.2`
- Workspace type: local Windows multi-root workspace, not Codespaces
- Session mode: Copilot Chat agent mode with tool calls

## What happened

A titled Copilot Chat session was missing from the VS Code/Copilot Chat session list.

On disk, the session is still represented in multiple places under:

```text
%APPDATA%\Code\User\workspaceStorage\<workspaceStorageId>\
```

The affected local session ID is a UUID. I can provide the exact ID and redacted file copies privately if useful.

## Storage evidence

### 1. `chat.ChatSessionStore.index` marks the session empty

In `%APPDATA%\Code\User\workspaceStorage\<workspaceStorageId>\state.vscdb`, key `chat.ChatSessionStore.index`, the affected session entry looks like this:

```json
{
  "sessionId": "<redacted-session-id>",
  "title": "<redacted-title>",
  "lastMessageDate": 1781323610996,
  "timing": {
    "created": 1781323610996
  },
  "initialLocation": "panel",
  "hasPendingEdits": false,
  "isEmpty": true,
  "isExternal": false,
  "lastResponseState": 1,
  "permissionLevel": "autoApprove"
}
```

Note that `isEmpty` is `true`, and `timing` has only `created`; there is no `lastRequestStarted` or `lastRequestEnded`, even though the transcript shows multiple user messages and assistant/tool events.

### 2. `chatSessions/<sessionId>.jsonl` is valid JSONL but only an empty stub

The corresponding UI-facing session file exists and parses as JSONL, but it has only 3 records.

Summary of the records:

```text
CHAT_SESSION_LINE_COUNT 3
1 kind 0 snapshotKeys [creationDate, customTitle, initialLocation, inputState, pendingRequests, requests, responderUsername, sessionId, version]
2 kind 1 keys [responderUsername]
3 kind 1 keys [hasPendingEdits]
```

The first snapshot has the title and session ID, but no requests:

```json
{
  "version": 3,
  "creationDate": 1781323610996,
  "customTitle": "<redacted-title>",
  "responderUsername": "",
  "sessionId": "<redacted-session-id>",
  "requests": [],
  "pendingRequests": []
}
```

### 3. The Copilot transcript for the same session is intact and non-empty

The corresponding transcript exists at:

```text
GitHub.copilot-chat\transcripts\<sessionId>.jsonl
```

It parses cleanly:

```text
TRANSCRIPT_EVENTS 557
BAD_JSON 0
TRANSCRIPT_COUNTS
{
  "session.start": 1,
  "user.message": 8,
  "assistant.turn_start": 87,
  "assistant.message": 84,
  "assistant.turn_end": 84,
  "tool.execution_start": 148,
  "tool.execution_complete": 145
}
```

So the session was definitely not empty.

### 4. The edit-state file also exists

The corresponding edit state also exists:

```text
chatEditingSessions\<sessionId>\state.json
```

It contains `initialFileContents`, timeline checkpoints, request IDs, and changed-file state.

### 5. The transcript ended mid-tool-call

The final transcript event is a `tool.execution_start` for `run_in_terminal`, with no matching `tool.execution_complete` before the transcript stops.

Sanitized shape of the final event:

```json
{
  "type": "tool.execution_start",
  "data": {
    "toolName": "run_in_terminal",
    "arguments": {
      "command": "<git status command over a small set of workspace files>",
      "explanation": "Inspect current state of root task/workspace files after the apparent reset.",
      "goal": "Inspect current workspace state",
      "mode": "sync",
      "timeout": 120000
    }
  },
  "timestamp": "2026-06-13T21:56:14.956Z"
}
```

This suggests the corruption may happen when the window/session is interrupted while a tool call is active or just after `tool.execution_start` is persisted.

### 6. There is evidence the `chatSessions` file used to be richer

A saved tool-output artifact from the same session contains search hits against the same `chatSessions/<sessionId>.jsonl` path at later line numbers such as 30, 33, 45, and 50, including entries under `requests` and `requests[*].response`.

That suggests the `chatSessions` file was not merely never written; it appears to have had request data at one point and later ended up as the 3-line empty stub.

## Expected behavior

If a session has any non-empty persisted local artifacts, the Chat history UI should not silently hide or classify it as empty.

Expected recovery behavior could be one or more of:

- Preserve the non-empty `chatSessions` snapshot/index atomically, even if a later rehydrate/write attempt sees an empty in-memory request list.
- Do not overwrite a non-empty `chatSessions/<sessionId>.jsonl` with an empty stub unless the user explicitly deletes/clears the chat.
- On startup, reconcile `chat.ChatSessionStore.index` with `GitHub.copilot-chat/transcripts` and `chatEditingSessions`; if transcript/edit state proves the session is non-empty, show it as recoverable/interrupted instead of hiding it.
- Provide a built-in “recover chat from transcript” command for cases where the transcript is intact but the UI session log/index is damaged.

## Actual behavior

- The session disappears from the normal Copilot/Chat session list.
- The index says `isEmpty: true`.
- The `chatSessions` file is a 3-line empty stub.
- The transcript and edit-state files prove substantial session content still exists.
- The only practical recovery is manual forensic extraction from `GitHub.copilot-chat/transcripts/<sessionId>.jsonl`, which is not a usable continuation path for normal users.

## Possible root cause hypothesis

This looks like a persistence race or non-atomic rewrite around session rehydration/interruption:

1. A long agent session is active.
2. A tool call starts and `tool.execution_start` is written to the Copilot transcript.
3. VS Code/window/session is interrupted before the tool completion/final assistant state is persisted.
4. On reload or cleanup, the Chat session store writes an empty/default snapshot (`requests: []`) and updates `chat.ChatSessionStore.index` as `isEmpty: true`, despite existing transcript/edit-state evidence.
5. The UI then treats the session as a blank/new chat and omits it from the useful history list.

I do not know whether the writer is VS Code core Chat persistence or the Copilot Chat extension, but the inconsistency spans both:

- Core-ish storage/index: `chatSessions/` and `chat.ChatSessionStore.index`
- Copilot storage: `GitHub.copilot-chat/transcripts/` and `GitHub.copilot-chat/debug-logs/`
- Edit state: `chatEditingSessions/`

## Diagnostic script used locally

This read-only Python snippet finds indexed sessions marked empty and compares them with `chatSessions`, Copilot transcripts, and edit state:

```python
import sqlite3, json, pathlib

root = pathlib.Path(r"%APPDATA%\Code\User\workspaceStorage\<workspaceStorageId>")
con = sqlite3.connect("file:" + str(root / "state.vscdb") + "?mode=ro", uri=True)
raw = con.execute("select value from ItemTable where key='chat.ChatSessionStore.index'").fetchone()[0]
data = json.loads(raw)

print("sessionId,title,isEmpty,chatLines,transcriptEvents,transcriptBadJson,hasEditState,lastMessageDate,created,lastRequestStarted,lastRequestEnded")

for sid, entry in sorted(data["entries"].items(), key=lambda kv: kv[1].get("timing", {}).get("created", 0)):
    if not entry.get("isEmpty"):
        continue

    chat = root / "chatSessions" / (sid + ".jsonl")
    transcript = root / "GitHub.copilot-chat" / "transcripts" / (sid + ".jsonl")
    edit_state = root / "chatEditingSessions" / sid / "state.json"

    chat_lines = sum(1 for _ in open(chat, encoding="utf-8")) if chat.exists() else -1
    transcript_events = 0
    transcript_bad_json = 0

    if transcript.exists():
        for line in open(transcript, encoding="utf-8"):
            try:
                json.loads(line)
                transcript_events += 1
            except Exception:
                transcript_bad_json += 1

    timing = entry.get("timing", {})
    print(",".join([
        sid,
        str(entry.get("title", "")),
        str(entry.get("isEmpty")),
        str(chat_lines),
        str(transcript_events),
        str(transcript_bad_json),
        str(edit_state.exists()),
        str(entry.get("lastMessageDate")),
        str(timing.get("created")),
        str(timing.get("lastRequestStarted")),
        str(timing.get("lastRequestEnded")),
    ]))
```

In this case it found one anomalous empty session:

```text
<title redacted>, isEmpty=True, chatLines=3, transcriptEvents=557, transcriptBadJson=0, hasEditState=True
```

## Questions for maintainers

1. Is `chatSessions/<sessionId>.jsonl` expected to be authoritative for Chat history, or should `GitHub.copilot-chat/transcripts/<sessionId>.jsonl` be considered a recoverable source of truth?
2. Is there a supported way to force VS Code/Copilot Chat to rehydrate a lost session from the transcript and edit-state files?
3. Would it be reasonable for startup/index loading to detect this inconsistency and surface a recoverable “interrupted session” entry?
4. What additional logs or traces should we capture the next time this happens?

Thank you. This is a painful failure mode because the data is still partly on disk, but the UI loses the session as a productive continuation unit.

This regressed again after upgrading 1.101.2 → 1.102.0, even with the notebook.output.fontFamily/fontSize workaround that previously fixed it:
https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/255475

title: Solid dark background color renders inconsistently on AMD GPU under native Wayland

body:
Type: Bug ## Steps to reproduce 1. Start VS Code under native Wayland (this is the default now) 2. Set the background color (`editor.background`) to `#16191D` ## Issue Now some regions of the editor are being rendered as `#15191C` or `#15191D` etc. instead of the expected value `#16191D`. The incorrectly rendered color differs from the expected color by exactly one value in one or more RGB channels. When scrolling the incorrectly rendered regions also move. ## How to fix When running VS Code under X11 (XWayland) or with GPU disabled (`--disable-gpu`) the issue disappears. So its likely an issue with GPU acceleration (?) ## Screenshots I attached two original screenshots and those exact same screenshots with `#16191D` replaced to make the differences easier to find in the original pictures. ### Screenshot 1 (original) ### Screenshot 1 (`#16191D` replaced) ### Screenshot 2 (original) ### Screenshot 2 (`#16191D` replaced) ## Notes I also confirmed this issue on a clean installation of Fedora Workstation 44 (GNOME) with a clean installation of VS Code and on archlinux (also GNOME). I don't know if this is the right place to report this issue, because I don't know if it's VS Code, Electron, Mutter, Wayland, MESA, amdgpu or whatever that's causing it, but I need to start somewhere.
